component_identification.content: Current Love Letter editions commonly include 21 character cards, 6 player reference cards, 13 favor tokens, and rules (some printings add a cloth bag). Older editions used 16 cards, so verify by edition before teaching. Round setup always removes one card face down from deck; in 2-player rules additional cards are often removed face up. Each active player starts a round with one card in hand and no hidden side components.

core_game_loop.content: On your turn, draw one card then choose one of your two cards to play, resolving its effect immediately. Effects may reveal information, force discards, protect a player, compare hands, swap hands, or eliminate players depending on role. Eliminated players are out for the round but return next round. Round ends when deck is empty or only one player remains. Award one favor token to the round winner and start the next round until someone reaches the required token threshold.

detailed_rules.content: Prepare deck per player count and edition instructions. Deal one card to each player. Turns are simple but timing-sensitive: draw, choose, play, resolve. Common role behavior across modern sets: Guard guesses another player’s role (not Guard) to eliminate if correct; investigative roles reveal/peek at hands; Baron-type effects compare hand values with lower eliminated; Handmaid-type protection prevents targeting until next turn; Prince-type effects force target discard and redraw; King-type swaps hands; Countess has forced-play condition when held with specific high nobility in many editions; Princess causes immediate self-elimination if discarded. All played cards remain visible in discard area, creating an information trail. A protected player cannot be targeted by most effects while protection lasts. If deck empties with multiple players alive, compare hand value; ties break via discard-value totals as specified in rules. Round winner gains one favor token. Continue rounds until a player reaches the player-count-specific token goal. Clarify edition differences before play because role lineup and forced-play text can differ. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Scoring is round-based via favor tokens. You gain one token by being the last player in round or by holding highest-value card when deck is exhausted. Tie-breaking at round end uses the prescribed comparison of discarded cards if needed. Full game ends when a player reaches target favor count for current player count (per printed chart in your edition). That player wins immediately.

beginner_strategy.content: Track every discard; Love Letter is mostly memory and probability. Don’t waste Guard guesses randomly—target roles that are statistically plausible from visible information. Time protective cards before high-risk turns when opponents can target you. Consider card valuation against deck size: with few cards left, safe survival may beat aggressive elimination attempts. In multiplayer, deny the current token leader when possible even if alternative plays seem marginally stronger short term. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.
